summary: Growing up, my family often listened to Joni Mitchell records at home, and I found myself really drawn to the music she made. Her songs were part of the early musical environment that inspired me to connect deeply with music. I would run around improvising bass lines inspired by what I heard, including Joni Mitchell's work. She was one of the artists that shaped my early musical sensibilities.

My early musical exposure included listening to Johann Strauss II, especially his Viennese waltzes. These classical pieces were part of the diverse range of music my family enjoyed at home. Even as a kid, I was fascinated by the bass parts in such works and would try to improvise around them. Strauss's music contributed to my initial desire to become a classical musician.

I grew up in Olympia, Washington, where the public school system allowed kids to start playing stringed instruments in fourth grade. Olympia was the place where I first got my bass and began my musical journey, including joining the jazz band program in middle school. It was also where I got involved in the local contra dance scene and played gigs at venues like the Capitol Theater and South Bay Grange. Olympia's music community was foundational to my early development as a musician.

When I was in middle school, I first heard Jaco Pastorius and it was a total brain explosion for me. His bass playing opened my eyes to new possibilities beyond classical music. I was so excited about his style that I even shared a compilation CD with a friend to introduce him to Jaco's work. Jaco became a huge influence on my approach to bass and improvisation.

Alongside discovering Jaco Pastorius, I also heard Weather Report for the first time during my middle school years. Their music was part of the jazz and fusion recordings that my teachers encouraged me to explore. Weather Report's innovative sound was a big part of what excited me about jazz and improvisation. They helped broaden my musical horizons beyond classical music.

I learned about Edgar Meyer around the same time I was discovering jazz fusion artists. Edgar Meyer became a huge bass hero of mine because of his work that blends classical and American folk traditions. His music introduced me to fiddle tunes and the world of contra dance music. Edgar's style inspired me to explore a wide range of musical genres on the bass.

One of my first experiences playing with professional musicians was when I sat in on a gig at the Capitol Theater in Olympia, where I played the bassline to Van Morrison's 'Moondance'. That moment was thrilling because I was just a kid, around 13 or 14, playing with seasoned players in a real theater setting. It was a big confidence boost and made me feel like I could be a professional musician. Van Morrison's music was part of that important early gig experience.

I remember hearing the Maceo Parker version of 'The Chicken' as part of the music that got me excited about bass and jazz funk. It was one of the tracks I shared with friends to introduce them to groove-based bass playing. That song, along with others, helped me appreciate the fun and rhythmic possibilities of bass lines. It was part of the music that inspired me to explore different styles.

Maceo Parker's music, especially his version of 'The Chicken,' was a big influence on me during my early jazz explorations. His funky, soulful style of playing bass and saxophone helped me understand the importance of groove and feel. I used his recordings to learn about rhythm and improvisation, and I even shared his music with friends to spread the excitement. Maceo's work was a key part of my musical education in middle school.

The Capitol Theater in Olympia was where I had one of my first real music experiences playing with professional musicians. Brad Schrandt, one of my teachers, had a gig there and invited me to sit in and play a tune. It was thrilling to perform on a real stage with an audience, and it made me feel like I belonged in the professional music world. The Capitol Theater holds a special place in my early career memories.

My first paid gig was subbing for the musical 'Forever Plaid' at the Capitol Playhouse in Olympia when I was about 13 years old. I played bass for the band behind the cast, which was a group of grown men singing doo-wop songs. Although I only rehearsed twice and didn't perform in the show, it was my first experience getting paid for music and feeling like I was working professionally. That gig was an important step in my musical journey.

I got deeply involved in the contra dance scene in Olympia during middle school, which introduced me to a vibrant community of dancers and musicians. Contra dance is a social dance with roots in New England and connections to English country dance, and it's very inclusive and welcoming. Playing bass for contra dances taught me to play simple but meaningful basslines and to experiment within the genre. It was a joyful and formative part of my musical life.

English country dance is closely related to the contra dance scene I was involved with in Olympia. Both forms are social dances with callers and easy-to-learn steps, and they share a tradition of fiddle tunes and group participation. My exposure to English country dance music came through playing in contra dance bands and learning fiddle tunes, which expanded my musical vocabulary. It was part of the folk dance culture that influenced my playing style.

Andrew Foster was a talented violin player I met in middle school who helped me with a project where I wrote music for a school play. He was also a fiddle player involved in the local contra dance scene, and I 'hired' him to play violin parts for my compositions. Andrew and his friends were starting a contra dance band and invited me to join as their bass player, which was a key moment in my early musical collaborations. He was an important musical peer during my formative years.

Centralia, Washington, is where Scotty, a friend of Andrew Foster's stepsister, lived. Scotty played piano and was part of the group of young musicians forming a contra dance band with Andrew and me. This connection helped expand the local music scene and provided opportunities to play gigs in the Olympia area. Centralia was part of the regional network of musicians I was involved with during my early career.

The South Bay Grange in Olympia was one of the venues where our young contra dance band played some of our first gigs. It was a community space that hosted dances and allowed us to experiment with fiddle tunes and simple basslines. Playing there helped me learn how to support dancers and contribute meaningfully to the music. The Grange was an important part of the local contra dance scene I was involved in.

Evergreen State College in Olympia was another venue where our contra dance band performed early on. It provided a supportive environment for folk and traditional music events, which helped nurture the local music community. Playing there gave me opportunities to develop my skills in fiddle tune styles and connect with other musicians and dancers. Evergreen was part of the musical ecosystem that shaped my early career.
The Retrospectacles were not mentioned in my story, so I don't have a direct connection or experience with them based on this transcript.

Seattle was mentioned as a nearby city but did not play a direct role in my early musical experiences described here. Most of my formative music activities happened in Olympia and surrounding areas. However, Seattle is part of the broader regional music scene that I eventually became aware of.

Portland, Oregon, was referenced as part of the regional context but was not directly involved in my early music career as described. My main activities were centered in Olympia and nearby towns. Portland represents part of the larger Northwest music environment I am familiar with.

Bellingham, Washington, was mentioned as part of the regional geography but did not feature in my early musical experiences. My focus was primarily on Olympia and the surrounding communities. Bellingham is part of the broader Pacific Northwest area connected to my musical background.
